<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1327792]] Wikidata name: Tamamo-no-Mae [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}
==Legends== Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.
[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|30em}}
==References== * {{cite web | title=Enjoying Otogizōshi with the Help of Synopsis and Illustrations, Ninth Story; Tamamo-No-Mae | website=Kyoto University Rare Materials Digital Archive| url=http://edb.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/exhibit-e/otogi/tamamo/tamamo.html | access-date=9 March 2022 |publisher=Kyoto University}} * {{cite web | title=Japanese Dakini | url=http://www.khandro.net/dakini_Japan.htm| access-date=22 February 2006 |website=Khandro}} * {{cite web |title=TAMANO-NO-MAE |url=https://www.godchecker.com/japanese-mythology/TAMAMO-NO-MAE/ |website=Godchecker |access-date=9 March 2022 |date=3 November 2018 |editor1-first=Chas |editor1-last=Saunders |editor2-first=Peter J. |editor2-last=Allen}} *{{cite thesis |last=Ferguson |first=Ian S. |date=2012 |title=The Evolution of a Legend: A Comparison of the Character of Tamamo no Mae Portrayed in Muromachi Period ''Otogizōshi'' and in the Late-Edo Vendetta Tale, ''Ito guruma kyūbi no kitsune'' (''The Spinning Wheel and the Nine-Tailed Fox'')|publisher=University of Colorado at Boulder |type=MA |series=Asian Languages & Civilizations Graduate Theses & Dissertations |volume=6 |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/54848969.pdf}} * {{cite book |last=Mailahn |first=Klaus |title=Der Fuchs in Glaube und Mythos |location=Münster, Germany |date=2006 |pages=190–194 |isbn=3-8258-9483-5 |publisher=LIT |language=de}} * {{cite book |last=Marks |first=Andreas |title=Japanese Yokai and Other Supernatural Beings |date=2023 |pages=107 |isbn=9781462923830 |publisher=Tuttle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uSudEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22nariko%22+tamamo+no+mae&pg=PA107}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1327792]] Wikidata name: Tamamo-no-Mae [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}
==Legends== Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.
[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|30em}}
==References== * {{cite web | title=Enjoying Otogizōshi with the Help of Synopsis and Illustrations, Ninth Story; Tamamo-No-Mae | website=Kyoto University Rare Materials Digital Archive| url=http://edb.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/exhibit-e/otogi/tamamo/tamamo.html | access-date=9 March 2022 |publisher=Kyoto University}} * {{cite web | title=Japanese Dakini | url=http://www.khandro.net/dakini_Japan.htm| access-date=22 February 2006 |website=Khandro}} * {{cite web |title=TAMANO-NO-MAE |url=https://www.godchecker.com/japanese-mythology/TAMAMO-NO-MAE/ |website=Godchecker |access-date=9 March 2022 |date=3 November 2018 |editor1-first=Chas |editor1-last=Saunders |editor2-first=Peter J. |editor2-last=Allen}} *{{cite thesis |last=Ferguson |first=Ian S. |date=2012 |title=The Evolution of a Legend: A Comparison of the Character of Tamamo no Mae Portrayed in Muromachi Period ''Otogizōshi'' and in the Late-Edo Vendetta Tale, ''Ito guruma kyūbi no kitsune'' (''The Spinning Wheel and the Nine-Tailed Fox'')|publisher=University of Colorado at Boulder |type=MA |series=Asian Languages & Civilizations Graduate Theses & Dissertations |volume=6 |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/54848969.pdf}} * {{cite book |last=Mailahn |first=Klaus |title=Der Fuchs in Glaube und Mythos |location=Münster, Germany |date=2006 |pages=190–194 |isbn=3-8258-9483-5 |publisher=LIT |language=de}} * {{cite book |last=Marks |first=Andreas |title=Japanese Yokai and Other Supernatural Beings |date=2023 |pages=107 |isbn=9781462923830 |publisher=Tuttle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uSudEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22nariko%22+tamamo+no+mae&pg=PA107}}
== External links == * {{commons Category-inline|Tamamo-no-Mae}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]]
{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}
==Legends== Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.
[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|30em}}
==References== * {{cite web | title=Enjoying Otogizōshi with the Help of Synopsis and Illustrations, Ninth Story; Tamamo-No-Mae | website=Kyoto University Rare Materials Digital Archive| url=http://edb.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/exhibit-e/otogi/tamamo/tamamo.html | access-date=9 March 2022 |publisher=Kyoto University}} * {{cite web | title=Japanese Dakini | url=http://www.khandro.net/dakini_Japan.htm| access-date=22 February 2006 |website=Khandro}} * {{cite web |title=TAMANO-NO-MAE |url=https://www.godchecker.com/japanese-mythology/TAMAMO-NO-MAE/ |website=Godchecker |access-date=9 March 2022 |date=3 November 2018 |editor1-first=Chas |editor1-last=Saunders |editor2-first=Peter J. |editor2-last=Allen}} *{{cite thesis |last=Ferguson |first=Ian S. |date=2012 |title=The Evolution of a Legend: A Comparison of the Character of Tamamo no Mae Portrayed in Muromachi Period ''Otogizōshi'' and in the Late-Edo Vendetta Tale, ''Ito guruma kyūbi no kitsune'' (''The Spinning Wheel and the Nine-Tailed Fox'')|publisher=University of Colorado at Boulder |type=MA |series=Asian Languages & Civilizations Graduate Theses & Dissertations |volume=6 |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/54848969.pdf}} * {{cite book |last=Mailahn |first=Klaus |title=Der Fuchs in Glaube und Mythos |location=Münster, Germany |date=2006 |pages=190–194 |isbn=3-8258-9483-5 |publisher=LIT |language=de}} * {{cite book |last=Marks |first=Andreas |title=Japanese Yokai and Other Supernatural Beings |date=2023 |pages=107 |isbn=9781462923830 |publisher=Tuttle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uSudEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22nariko%22+tamamo+no+mae&pg=PA107}}
== External links == * {{commons Category-inline|Tamamo-no-Mae}}

{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan|1=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko|1=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion|1=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}
==Legends== Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit|1=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|1=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.
[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si|1=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You|1=King You of Zhou}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu|1=Nasu, Tochigi}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|30em}}
==References== * {{cite web | title=Enjoying Otogizōshi with the Help of Synopsis and Illustrations, Ninth Story; Tamamo-No-Mae | website=Kyoto University Rare Materials Digital Archive| url=http://edb.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/exhibit-e/otogi/tamamo/tamamo.html | access-date=9 March 2022 |publisher=Kyoto University}} * {{cite web | title=Japanese Dakini | url=http://www.khandro.net/dakini_Japan.htm| access-date=22 February 2006 |website=Khandro}} * {{cite web |title=TAMANO-NO-MAE |url=https://www.godchecker.com/japanese-mythology/TAMAMO-NO-MAE/ |website=Godchecker |access-date=9 March 2022 |date=3 November 2018 |editor1-first=Chas |editor1-last=Saunders |editor2-first=Peter J. |editor2-last=Allen}} *{{cite thesis |last=Ferguson |first=Ian S. |date=2012 |title=The Evolution of a Legend: A Comparison of the Character of Tamamo no Mae Portrayed in Muromachi Period ''Otogizōshi'' and in the Late-Edo Vendetta Tale, ''Ito guruma kyūbi no kitsune'' (''The Spinning Wheel and the Nine-Tailed Fox'')|publisher=University of Colorado at Boulder |type=MA |series=Asian Languages & Civilizations Graduate Theses & Dissertations |volume=6 |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/54848969.pdf}} * {{cite book |last=Mailahn |first=Klaus |title=Der Fuchs in Glaube und Mythos |location=Münster, Germany |date=2006 |pages=190–194 |isbn=3-8258-9483-5 |publisher=LIT |language=de}} * {{cite book |last=Marks |first=Andreas |title=Japanese Yokai and Other Supernatural Beings |date=2023 |pages=107 |isbn=9781462923830 |publisher=Tuttle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uSudEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22nariko%22+tamamo+no+mae&pg=PA107}}
== External links == * {{commons Category-inline|Tamamo-no-Mae}}

{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan|1=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko|1=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion|1=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}

[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si|1=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You|1=King You of Zhou}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu|1=Nasu, Tochigi}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]]
{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan|1=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko|1=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion|1=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}
==Legends== Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit|1=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|1=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.
[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si|1=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You|1=King You of Zhou}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu|1=Nasu, Tochigi}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|30em}}
==References== * {{cite web | title=Enjoying Otogizōshi with the Help of Synopsis and Illustrations, Ninth Story; Tamamo-No-Mae | website=Kyoto University Rare Materials Digital Archive| url=http://edb.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/exhibit-e/otogi/tamamo/tamamo.html | access-date=9 March 2022 |publisher=Kyoto University}} * {{cite web | title=Japanese Dakini | url=http://www.khandro.net/dakini_Japan.htm| access-date=22 February 2006 |website=Khandro}} * {{cite web |title=TAMANO-NO-MAE |url=https://www.godchecker.com/japanese-mythology/TAMAMO-NO-MAE/ |website=Godchecker |access-date=9 March 2022 |date=3 November 2018 |editor1-first=Chas |editor1-last=Saunders |editor2-first=Peter J. |editor2-last=Allen}} *{{cite thesis |last=Ferguson |first=Ian S. |date=2012 |title=The Evolution of a Legend: A Comparison of the Character of Tamamo no Mae Portrayed in Muromachi Period ''Otogizōshi'' and in the Late-Edo Vendetta Tale, ''Ito guruma kyūbi no kitsune'' (''The Spinning Wheel and the Nine-Tailed Fox'')|publisher=University of Colorado at Boulder |type=MA |series=Asian Languages & Civilizations Graduate Theses & Dissertations |volume=6 |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/54848969.pdf}} * {{cite book |last=Mailahn |first=Klaus |title=Der Fuchs in Glaube und Mythos |location=Münster, Germany |date=2006 |pages=190–194 |isbn=3-8258-9483-5 |publisher=LIT |language=de}} * {{cite book |last=Marks |first=Andreas |title=Japanese Yokai and Other Supernatural Beings |date=2023 |pages=107 |isbn=9781462923830 |publisher=Tuttle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uSudEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22nariko%22+tamamo+no+mae&pg=PA107}}
== External links == * {{commons Category-inline|Tamamo-no-Mae}}

One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. [[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[de:Tamamo no Mae]][[en:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[es:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[fr:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[hu:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[id:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[it:Tamamo no Mae]][[ja:玉藻前]][[jv:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ko:다마모노마에]][[pt:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[ru:Тамамо-но маэ]][[simple:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[vi:Tamamo-no-Mae]][[zh:玉藻前]]
{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}} [[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]
'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan|1=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).
Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko|1=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion|1=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}}
==Legends== Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit|1=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|1=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.
[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]] In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si|1=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You|1=King You of Zhou}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.
The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu|1=Nasu, Tochigi}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}
[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]] In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}}
The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|30em}}
==References== * {{cite web | title=Enjoying Otogizōshi with the Help of Synopsis and Illustrations, Ninth Story; Tamamo-No-Mae | website=Kyoto University Rare Materials Digital Archive| url=http://edb.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/exhibit-e/otogi/tamamo/tamamo.html | access-date=9 March 2022 |publisher=Kyoto University}} * {{cite web | title=Japanese Dakini | url=http://www.khandro.net/dakini_Japan.htm| access-date=22 February 2006 |website=Khandro}} * {{cite web |title=TAMANO-NO-MAE |url=https://www.godchecker.com/japanese-mythology/TAMAMO-NO-MAE/ |website=Godchecker |access-date=9 March 2022 |date=3 November 2018 |editor1-first=Chas |editor1-last=Saunders |editor2-first=Peter J. |editor2-last=Allen}} *{{cite thesis |last=Ferguson |first=Ian S. |date=2012 |title=The Evolution of a Legend: A Comparison of the Character of Tamamo no Mae Portrayed in Muromachi Period ''Otogizōshi'' and in the Late-Edo Vendetta Tale, ''Ito guruma kyūbi no kitsune'' (''The Spinning Wheel and the Nine-Tailed Fox'')|publisher=University of Colorado at Boulder |type=MA |series=Asian Languages & Civilizations Graduate Theses & Dissertations |volume=6 |url=https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/54848969.pdf}} * {{cite book |last=Mailahn |first=Klaus |title=Der Fuchs in Glaube und Mythos |location=Münster, Germany |date=2006 |pages=190–194 |isbn=3-8258-9483-5 |publisher=LIT |language=de}} * {{cite book |last=Marks |first=Andreas |title=Japanese Yokai and Other Supernatural Beings |date=2023 |pages=107 |isbn=9781462923830 |publisher=Tuttle|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uSudEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22nariko%22+tamamo+no+mae&pg=PA107}}

{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}}
[[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka  [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]

'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan|1=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).

Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko|1=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion|1=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}} 

==Legends==
Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit|1=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|1=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.

[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]]
In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si|1=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You|1=King You of Zhou}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.

The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu|1=Nasu, Tochigi}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}

[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]]
In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}} 

The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>

{{Short description|Legendary figure in Japanese mythology}}
[[Image:Tamamo-no-mae-woodblock.jpg|right|thumb|Tamamo-no-Mae Woodblock print by Tsukioka  [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]]]

'''Tamamo-no-Mae''' ({{lang|ja|玉藻前}}, {{lang|ja|玉藻の前}}, also {{lang|ja|玉藻御前}}) is a legendary figure in [[Japanese mythology]]. One of the stories explaining the legend comes from {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} (1336 to 1573) genre fiction called ''[[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]]''. In the otogizōshi Tamamo-no-Mae was a {{ill|Courtesan|en|Courtesan|WD=Q376924|lt=Courtesan|1=Courtesan}} under the Japanese {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}} (who reigned from 1142 through 1155).

Tamamo-no-Mae is believed to have been based on the historical empress consort {{ill|Fujiwara no Nariko|en|Fujiwara no Nariko|WD=Q5507535|lt=Fujiwara no Nariko|1=Fujiwara no Nariko}} (1117-1160), mother of Konoe, who participated in a number of succession struggles leading to the {{ill|Hōgen rebellion|en|Hōgen rebellion|WD=Q842357|lt=Hōgen rebellion|1=Hōgen rebellion}} and {{ill|Heiji rebellion|en|Heiji rebellion|WD=Q944859|lt=Heiji rebellion|1=Heiji rebellion}}.{{sfnp|Marks|2023|p=107}} 

==Legends==
Stories of Tamamo-no-Mae being a legendary {{ill|Fox spirit|en|Fox spirit|WD=Q1424792|lt=Fox spirit|1=Fox spirit}} appear during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} as [[:en:Otogizōshi|Otogizōshi]] (prose narratives), and were also mentioned by [[Toriyama Sekien]] in ''{{ill|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|en|Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|WD=Q2703425|lt=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi|1=Konjaku Hyakki Shūi}}''. Edo period folklore then conflated the legend with similar foreign stories about fox spirits corrupting rulers, causing chaos in their territories.

[[File:Hokusai_Sangoku_Yoko-den.jpg|thumb|300px|Fleeing fox spirit as Lady Kayō depicted in Hokusai's ''Sangoku Yōko-den'' ({{lang|ja|三国妖狐伝}})]]
In the story told by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, formed in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the nine-tailed fox first appeared in China and possessed {{ill|Daji|en|Daji|WD=Q1377681|lt=Daji|1=Daji}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}}'s last ruler {{ill|King Zhou of Shang|en|King Zhou of Shang|WD=Q470072|lt=King Zhou|1=Di Xin}}. She enchanted the king and brought on a reign of terror that led to a rebellion that ended the Shang dynasty. The fox spirit fled to [[:en:Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]] of {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|WD=Q3179625|lt=Tianzhu|1=Tianzhu (India)}} (ancient India) and became Lady Kayō ({{lang|ja|華陽夫人}}), concubine of the crown prince Banzoku ({{lang|ja|班足太子}}; based on Indian tales of {{ill|Kalmashapada|en|Kalmashapada|WD=Q17006691|lt=Kalmashapada|1=Kalmashapada}} the man-eater),{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3|loc=note 3}} causing him to cut off the heads of a thousand men. It was then defeated again, and fled the country. The same fox returned to China around 780 BC and was said to have possessed {{ill|Bao Si|en|Bao Si|WD=Q1069735|lt=Bao Si|1=Bao Si}}, a concubine of the {{ill|Zhou dynasty|en|Zhou dynasty|WD=Q35216|lt=Zhou dynasty|1=Zhou dynasty}} {{ill|King You of Zhou|en|King You of Zhou|WD=Q1193397|lt=King You|1=King You of Zhou}}. It was again chased away by human military forces.

The fox stayed quiet for some time. Then she appeared in Japan as Tamamo-no-Mae, the most favoured courtesan of {{ill|Emperor Konoe|en|Emperor Konoe|WD=Q373997|lt=Emperor Konoe|1=Emperor Konoe}}. She was said to be a most beautiful and intelligent woman, being able to answer any question asked. She caused the Emperor to be extremely ill and was eventually exposed as a fox spirit by the astrologer Abe no Yasuchika, who had been called to diagnose the cause of the Emperor's poor health. A few years later, the emperor sent Kazusa-no-suke ({{lang|ja|上総介}}) and Miura-no-suke ({{lang|ja|三浦介}}) to kill the fox in the plains of {{ill|Nasu, Tochigi|en|Nasu, Tochigi|WD=Q636416|lt=Nasu|1=Nasu, Tochigi}}.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|pp=2-3}}

[[File:Jizo,Kshitigarbha,Sesshoseki_,Nasu_Town,Tochigi_Prefecture,Japan_20160817.jpg|thumb|200px|{{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki|1=Sessho-seki}} (Killing Stone) and Thousand {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}} Statues]]
In the 1653 ''Tamamo no sōshi'' ({{lang|ja|玉藻の草紙}}), an addendum was added to the story describing that the spirit of Tamamo-no-mae embedded itself into a stone called the ''[[:en:Sesshō-seki|Sesshō-seki]]''. The stone continually released poisonous gas, killing everything that touched it.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=3}} The stone was said to have been destroyed in the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} by the Buddhist monk Gennō Shinshō ({{lang|ja|源翁心昭}}), who exorcised the now-repentant fox spirit. He held a Buddhist memorial service after the deed, allowing the spirit to finally rest in peace.{{sfnp|Ferguson|2012|p=4}} 

The stone split in two on March 5, 2022.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=殺生石 真っ二つ 以前からひび、自然現象か 那須｜社会,県内主要｜下野新聞「SOON」ニュース｜下野新聞 SOON(スーン) |url=https://www.shimotsuke.co.jp/articles/-/561829 |access-date=2022-03-06 |website=下野新聞 SOON |language=ja}}</ref> Cracks in the stone had been seen several years before the split, possibly allowing rainwater to penetrate and weaken it, so it is highly likely that the stone cracked naturally.<ref name=":0" /> Some humorously speculated that this had freed Tamamo-no-Mae, and social media in Japan predicted that dark forces had been released.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Japan's 'killing stone' splits in two, releasing superstitions amid the sulphur springs |last=McCurry |first=Justin |newspaper=The Guardian |date=7 March 2022 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/mar/07/japans-killing-stone-splits-in-two-releasing-superstitions-and-toxic-gases}}</ref>
